course_description: This course is an introductory level examination of the law regulating business in Australia today for accountants, business managers and other non-legal professionals. The course seeks to develop students' knowledge of the Australian legal system and of the laws associated with starting, managing, financing and closing a business, as well as their ability to solve simple legal problems.

requirements.restrictions: Not to be studied by LLB, LLB dual, LLB(Hons) or LLB(Hons) dual students
requirements.assumed_background: LAWS1100 Business Law is an introductory level law course. No prior study of law is required or assumed.

timetable:
Timetables are available on mySI-net.

Additional Timetable Information
Please Note:

LECTURES

Lectures will be held in Teaching Weeks 1 - 7 and Teaching Weeks 9-13.

Due to the Easter Public Holiday and timing of the Problem Set Scenario question, there will be no lectures in Teaching Week 8.

TUTORIALS

Tutorials will be held in Teaching Weeks 2, 4 - 7 and 9 - 13.

There will be no Tutorial held in Teaching Weeks 1, 3 or 8.

Next 2. Aims
course_aims: The aim of LAWS1100 is to give you a broad knowledge and understanding of Australian Business Law in order to be able to recognise, resolve and avoid legal problems in your business and commercial dealings.

learning_outcomes.description: Identify and locate relevant legal information in the field of Australian Business Law.
Recognise Business Law contexts and situations which potentially give rise to legal issues or problems.

Apply legal rules and principles (such as rules to do with contract, tort, intellectual property and consumer law) to assess your legal position in relation to typical business and commercial dealings.
Plan and produce written answers to problem based legal questions for your instructors and fellow students.
Evaluate your legal, social and professional responsibilities to your peers, your profession and your community.

assessment_summary.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_summary.title: Multiple-choice test
assessment_summary.due_date: One of the Saturdays set aside for mid-semester exams: 30 March, 6 April or 13 April

assessment_details.title: Multiple-choice test
assessment_details.category: Exam - Mid Semester Outside Scheduled Class
assessment_details.due_date: One of the Saturdays set aside for mid-semester exams: 30 March, 6 April or 13 April
assessment_details.task_description:
The Mid-Semester Exam is a CLOSED Book Exam and is weighted at 30% of your total mark for the Course.

The Test will consist of 30 multiple-choice questions relating to the material covered in Teaching Weeks 1 & 2.

You will have 10 minutes perusal and then 60 minutes to complete the Test.

In Tutorial 2, a practice exam consisting of 10 x multiple choice questions (of similar difficulty to those questions asked in the Mid Semester Exam) will be given to students. Only those who attend Tutorials will have access to this material.

Unmarked Hard-copy Bilingual Dictionaries will be permitted.
assessment_details.criteria_and_marking:
UQ Students: Please access the profile from
Learn.UQ

or
mySI-net

to access marking criteria held in this profile.

assessment_details.title: Tutorial preparation and participation
assessment_details.task_description:
There are ten (10) tutorials in total for this course. Attendance at and preparation for all tutorials is strongly recommended . Each tutorial contains a question that students should answer in writing before attending the relevant tutorial. The purpose of this is: (1) to assist you to practice structuring your work in preparation for further course assessment in LAWS1100, (2) to assist with your preparation for tutorials and (3) to encourage active group participation by all individuals in tutorials.

You will have the opportunity to obtain feedback in relation to your answers to the tutorial tasks throughout the tutorials. The answer to each task will be examined in the relevant tutorial at which time you will be able to ask questions and discuss the answer. It is each student's responsibility to add to or amend their own written tutorial answer based on the class discussion.

You will not be provided with detailed written feedback about your individual answer to the tutorial tasks.

There will be SEVEN (7) assessable Tutorials. For FIVE (5) of these tutorials (it is your choice which 5 you complete), you must submit a 1-2 A4 page answer to the specified tutorial task. To be eligible for the written preparation marks for a particular week's tutorial, you must submit your written preparation to your tutor at the commencement of the relevant tutorial. You can NOT type your submission. You must also remain in the tutorial for the full 50 minutes - you cannot submit your work and then leave.

Please NOTE that the 7 assessable Tutorials are:

Tutorial 1

Tutorial 4

Tutorial 5

Tutorial 6

Tutorial 8

Tutorial 9

This means that Tutorials 2, 3 and 7 are not assessable. (Note: Tutorial 7 contains a 5% submission which is discussed in more detail under the "Problem Set Scenario" assessment item.)

If you submit your written preparation for a week's tutorial at the commencement of the tutorial and your preparation demonstrates a reasonable attempt at the specified task, you will be awarded 1 mark. To receive the 1 mark, your answers do not have to be correct but must contain sufficient material to demonstrate a reasonable attempt.

A maximum of 5 marks for the whole semester will be awarded for this form of assessment.

Submission: presented to the tutor at commencement of the relevant tutorial. Your written answer must be shown to your tutor in class and cannot be submitted (by email or otherwise) between classes.

assessment_details.title: Problem scenario + answer analysis exercise
assessment_details.task_description:
This assessment item will reflect largely the calibre and style of question that will be expected of students in the Final Examination. As such, s tudents will be graded out of 15 (15% of the Course) for this piece of assessment.

A problem question (based on materials already covered in lectures) will be released to students at 5.00pm on Thursday 11th April. Students will have approximately 4 days to submit their response, using the ILAC technique.

Submission: Your written answer must be submitted via blackboard by the specified time and date - 2.00pm on Monday 15th April.

To provide students with extensive feedback on their 15% submission
, the answer to this question, will be discussed in detail

, as part of the Tutorial program (in Tutorial 8).

There will
also be a 5% reflective, answer-evaluation exercise that will be due in Tutorial 8 (Teaching Week 11)

. This will not be graded according to whether the student is
'right' or 'wrong'. S

tudents will be awarded 5%, by their Tutor, for demonstrating that they have put in a good effort in response to this exercise. More details will be provided during the Semester about this 5% assessment.

Assignments must be submitted electronically in both Word and PDF via the ‘Online Submission of Assignments’ folder on the course Blackboard site.

assessment_details.title: Final exam
assessment_details.task_description:
The Final Examination will be an OPEN book examination that covers all materials in Teaching Wks 3-13. The Final Exam will NOT cover the materials in Teaching Wks 1 and 2.

The exam will consist of 3 x ILAC "problem style" questions. Each question is worth 15 marks and will be of a similar difficulty level to what is asked in the Tutorials and assessable and non assessable Problem Set Questions.

learning_resources.required_resources:
James, Nickolas, Business Law (Wiley, 4th edition, 2017).

Material prescribed via the course Blackboard website.
learning_resources.university_learning_resources:
Access to required and recommended resources, plus past central exam papers, is available at the UQ Library website ( http://www.library.uq.edu.au/lr/LAWS1100 ).

The University offers a range of resources and services to support student learning. Details are available on the myUQ website ( https://my.uq.edu.au/ ).
learning_resources.school_learning_resources:
Law School Citation Style

Students preparing assignments, research papers, dissertations and theses are required to follow the Australian Guide to Legal Citation (4th edition) .

For a short summary of the complete Australian Guide to Legal Citation , see the UQ Library's Referencing Guide to the AGLC .

Law Library

The Law Library's Legal Research Guide site at http://guides.library.uq.edu.au/legal-research-guide provides links to a wide range of Australian and International sites relevant to the study of law.

Students should refer to the UQ Library website generally for information relating to facilities, collections and services available - http://www.library.uq.edu.au .

Course Learn. UQ (Blackboard) Website

Course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites contain course announcements; the course profile; the learning guide (where applicable); lecture slides and discussion boards. Students should visit their course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites regularly to check for course announcements and to read and contribute to any online activities.

Course Coordinators will maintain and regularly update course Learn.UQ (Blackboard) websites, which can be accessed via the following link – https://learn.uq.edu.au/ . It is your responsibility to ensure that you have access to the websites . Access to Learn.UQ (Blackboard) is automatically generated for those students enrolled through my SI-net approximately 1 week prior to the course commencing. If you do not have access by that time please contact the AskUs Library Help – askus@library.uq.edu.au .

The University of Queensland Library offers computing help and training free to UQ students - https://web.library.uq.edu.au/library-services/it .

Previous 2. Aims

Next 4. Activities

learning_activities.topic: Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 1 - Intro & Australian Legal System : Part 1: Course overview and introduction. The first half of the lecture will provide you with an overview of the Course and will outline any significant changes that have been made recently to LAWS1100. Assessment items will also be discussed in detail.
Mon - Fri No Tutorials : There are no tutorials in Teaching Week 1.

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 2 - Sources of Law & the Constitution : This lecture will expand upon many of the theoretical areas discussed in Teaching Week 1 and, in particular, we will examine the sources of Australian Law (Common Law and Legislation), the process of Statutory Interpretation and many important aspects of the Australian Constitution. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

Mon - Fri Tutorial 1 - The Australian Legal System : (ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based mainly on the material from the Teaching Wk 1 Lecture. However, students should also read the Chapter in the textbook that discusses the Sources of Australian Law, ahead of their Tutorial, in order to answer each question and be awarded the 1% Tutorial mark.

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 3 - Tort Law (1) & the ILAC Method : This lecture examines the Tort of Negligence. In particular, we will look at the 3 major elements of negligence (duty of care, breach of duty & causation). Defences for negligence will also be discussed. At the end of the lecture, we will also be discussing and analysing at the ILAC Method (the method used to answer legal problems this Semester). This will be in preparation for the Non Assessable Problem Set Scenario Question in Tutorial 3.
Mon - Fri No Tutorials : There are no Tutorials in Teaching Week 3.

Fri 9:00 NON GRADED Problem Set Question (Non Assessable) : Practice Problem Set Question (Release Date: Friday 15 March, 2019): This is not for assessment and is not compulsory. However this problem style question is of a similar level of difficulty to the type of question that will be asked in both the Graded Problem Set Assessment (20%) and the Final Examination (45%). For those students wanting to practice their skills in providing a quality legal answer using the ILAC method, this is a great opportunity to do so. Students will be provided with detailed feedback on this Problem Set Question in Tutorial 3. All Tutors will also be allocating some time to give brief individual feedback to those students who have attempted an answer.

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 4 - Tort Law (2) & Contract Law (1) : The first half of this lecture examines all other types of Torts actions (the Tort of Negligence was discussed in the previous lecture). This includes the Tort of Trespass, the Tort of Defamation and Deceit and Negligent Misstatement. The second half of the lecture will then commence with Contract Law - in particular focusing on the "Primary Elements of a Contract" (agreement, intention and consideration).

Mon - Fri Tutorial 2 - Sources of Law & the Constitution : (NON ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wk 2 Lecture. The second half of this Tutorial will provide students with an opportunity to sit a practice 10 question, multiple choice exam (similar in style to the Mid Semester Examination).

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 5 - Contract Law (2) : This lecture will examine Contract Law in greater detail. It is likely that at the beginning of this lecture we will finish off any areas still outstanding from Contract 1. After that, we will then go on to identify what does and does not constitute a term of a contract (including how the Parole Evidence Rule affects this). We will also examine other important areas of Contract Law such as promissory estoppel, contractual capacity and disclaimers/exclusion clauses.

Mon - Fri Tutorial 3 - Tort Law : (NON ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wk 3 Lecture. This Tutorial will give students detailed feedback on the Practice Problem Set Question (which is based upon the Tort of Negligence) that was released on Friday 15 March.

Mon - Fri NON GRADED Problem Set Question (Non Assessable) : The Non Graded Problem Set Question will be discussed in Tutorial 3. Detailed feedback will be given to students on this question, including how best to answer the question using the ILAC technique. All Tutors will also be allocating some time to give brief individual feedback to those students who have attempted an answer.

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 6 - ILAC Seminar & Contract Law 3 : The lecture will commence with a workshop on how to answer ILAC problems in this subject. The workshop will analyse a question based on the law to do with disclaimers/exclusion clauses (which was discussed at the end of the Teaching Wk 6 Lecture). Various answers to that question will be provided, ranging from very high quality answers, to low quality answers. After the ILAC Seminar, the lecture will then focus on the 5 Vitiating Factors that can lead to a contract being terminated for lack of free and voluntary consent. These are: misrepresentation, mistake, undue influence, duress and unconscionable conduct. Contract Law remedies will also be discussed, as will the ways in which a contract can be brought to an end (including discharge by frustration).

Mon - Fri Tutorial 4 - Contract 1 : (ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the second half of the Teaching Wk 4 Lecture. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 7 - The Law of Agency : This lecture will examine the law to do with principals and their agents and the special relationship that they share. The fiduciary duty that an agent owes to his/her principal will also be discussed. Note: This topic is closely related to the area of "partnership" that will be discussed in the "Business Structures" lecture, at the end of the Semester.

Mon - Fri Tutorial 5 - Contract 2&3 : (ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wk 5&6 Lectures. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

Thu 17:00 - Mon 14:00 GRADED PROBLEM SET ASSESSMENT (Problem Set) : Your answer will be graded out of 15 and will be worth 15% of the final grade for LAWS1100. The question will be released on Thursday 11th April at 5.00pm. Your ILAC answer MUST be submitted by Monday 15th April at 2.00pm.
Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 8 - No Lectures : There are no Lectures or Tutorials in Teaching Week 8, due to the Easter Public Holiday.
Mon - Fri No Tutorials - Teaching Week 8 : There are no Lectures or Tutorials in Teaching Week 8, due to the Easter Public Holiday.

Thu 17:00 - Mon 14:00 GRADED PROBLEM SET ASSESSMENT (Problem Set) : Your answer will be graded out of 15 and will be worth 15% of the final grade for LAWS1100. The question will be released on Thursday 11th April at 5.00pm. Your ILAC answer MUST be submitted by Monday 15th April at 2.00pm.
Mon - Fri Mid Semester Break - No Lectures : There are no Lectures this week, due to the Mid Semester break.
Mon - Fri Mid Semester Break - No Tutorials : There are no tutorials this week, due to the Mid Semester break.

Mon - Sun Teaching Wk 9 - Intellectual Property : This lecture will examine Intellectual Property Law. The first half of the lecture will look predominantly at the law of Copyright (both in terms of Copyright protection and Copyright infringement). In the second half of the lecture, we will discuss other areas of Intellectual Property Law, including Trademarks, Patents, Design and Commercial Secret.

Mon - Fri Tutorial 6 - Law of Agency : (ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wk 7 Lecture. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 10 - Competition and Consumer Law 1 : This lecture will be the first of 2 lectures that investigate the Competition and Consumer Act. The lectures will be divided into two main areas: the first focusing on a Schedule to the Competition and Consumer Act called the ACL (Australian Consumer Law) and the second focusing on anti-competitive provisions under the Act itself. This week, we will examine the areas of Misleading and Deceptive Conduct, Unconscionable Conduct, Unfair Terms, False Representations and Implied Consumer Guarantees, that fall under the Australian Consumer Law (ACL) schedule.

Mon - Fri Tutorial 7 - Intellectual Property Law : (ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wk 9 Lecture. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

Mon - Fri Teaching Week 11 - Competition and Consumer Law 2 : This lecture is the second lecture that investigates the Competition and Consumer Act. In the first half of the lecture, we will finish examining the areas of Misleading and Deceptive Conduct, Unconscionable Conduct, Unfair Terms, False Representations and Implied Consumer Guarantees, that fall under the Australian Consumer Law (ACL) schedule. The second half of the lecture will then discuss anti-competitive behaviour provisions under the Competition and Consumer Act 2010 - that lead to substantially lessening competition in the marketplace.

Mon - Fri Tutorial 8 - Contracts 2 : (5% ASSESSMENT ITEM SUBMITTED IN THIS TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wk 5 Lecture and will provide students with detailed feedback on the 15% Assessable ILAC Problem Question that was due on Monday 15th April 2019 at 2.00pm. Students will also be asked to prepare a 5% reflective answer-evaluation exercise, again related to the Problem Set Scenario question. Your attempt at this exercise will have to be shown to your Tutor at the start of this Tutorial. This assessment item will be discussed further in Lecture 1 and also closer to this Tutorial.

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 12 - Business Structures : In this lecture, we will look at the different types of key business structures in Australia, including Sole Traders, Partnerships and Corporations. In particular, we will focus on the area of 'partnerships', which is closely related to the Law of Agency (that was discussed in Teaching Wk 7). Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

Mon - Fri Tutorial 9 - Competition & Consumer Law 1 & 2 : (ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wks 10 and 11 Lectures. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

Mon - Fri Teaching Wk 13 - Revision and Exam Preparation : The final lecture will be aimed at preparing students for the Final Examination. This lecture will discuss all the details to do with the Final Examination and will also provide students with a brief summary of all the material that has been covered throughout the Semester. Students will also have the opportunity to ask any questions that they may have about the Final Exam. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 5

Mon - Fri Tutorial 10 - Business Structures : (ASSESSABLE TUTORIAL) This Tutorial will be based on the material from the Teaching Wk 12 Lecture. Learning Objectives: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5 Readings/Ref: Wiley; Blackboard

policies_and_procedures:
University Policies & Guidelines

An overview of the University's assessment-related procedures can be found on my.UQ. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/services/exams-and-assessment )

Academic Integrity

It is the University's task to encourage ethical scholarship and to inform students and staff about the institutional standards of academic behaviour expected of them in learning, teaching and research. Students have a responsibility to maintain the highest standards of academic integrity in their work. Students must not cheat in examinations or other forms of assessment and must ensure they do not plagiarise.

Academic Integrity Tutorial - Support for Students

It may seem simple – don’t cheat and do reference your work. However, there are many issues involved in understanding what is meant by academic integrity and how you could be misled into engaging in an action which may be academic misconduct. The Academic Integrity Tutorial (AIT) will enable you to uphold academic integrity throughout your studies at UQ and beyond. All students are strongly encouraged to complete the module. Some course coordinators may require you to complete the module as part of your assessment.

Plagiarism

The University has adopted the following definition of plagiarism:

Plagiarism is the act of misrepresenting as one's own original work the ideas, interpretations, words or creative works of another. These include published and unpublished documents, designs, music, sounds, images, photographs, computer codes and ideas gained through working in a group. These ideas, interpretations, words or works may be found in print and/or electronic media.

Students are encouraged to read the UQ Student Integrity and Misconduct policy ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.60.04-student-integrity-and-misconduct ) which makes a comprehensive statement about the University's approach to plagiarism, including the approved use of plagiarism detection software, the consequences of plagiarism and the principles associated with preventing plagiarism.

Applications for Extensions

An application for an Extension of Assessment Due Date for medical grounds and/or other exceptional circumstances shall be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation by the date specified in Section 5.3 of the Electronic Course Profile. Students will be notified of the outcome of their request via the myRequests section of my.UQ (and an email sent to their student account).

Additional details associated with extension requests, including acceptable and unacceptable reasons, may be found at my.UQ .

Request for Deferral of Exams

A request for deferral of an exam must be submitted by lodging the appropriate (online) form with supporting documentation via mySI-net > my Requests no later than five (5) calendar days after the date of the original exam. Students are encouraged to familiarise themselves with the information on deferring an exam available on my.UQ .

Feedback on Assessment

There are certain steps you can take if you feel your result does not reflect your performance. Please refer to the my.UQ web site. ( https://my.uq.edu.au/information-and-services/manage-my-program/academic-progress-and-final-results/querying-result )

As a student you have a responsibility to incorporate feedback into your learning; make use of the assessment criteria that you are given; be aware of the rules, policies and other documents related to assessment; and provide teachers with feedback on their assessment practices.

Supplementary assessment

A supplementary assessment is designed to give you a second chance at achieving a passing grade for a course, should you receive a final course grade of 3 (or N subject to the Supplementary Assessment Procedures ). It isn't a repeat of an exam, or a specific assignment – it's designed to test the learning outcomes for the entire course, or specific learning outcomes tailored to you.

Supplementary assessment can take any form (e.g. oral, written, examination etc.), and the highest grade you can receive is a 4 (or P). Supplementary assessment isn't available for every course and is only granted in accordance with the Supplementary Assessment Procedures .

Please check the course profile to see if your course is fully or partially exempt from supplementary assessment. If you have any questions about supplementary assessment, or if you're unsure whether you qualify, please contact staff in your school office or faculty office.

Course grade cut-offs

Any change to grade cut-offs will be in accordance with section 4.7.5 of PPL 3.10.02 Assessment – Procedures .

School of Law
Assessment Guidelines

Communication with Examiners

Students who have completed an item of assessment should not contact the examiners about that item of assessment prior to the release of the grades or marks for that item of assessment. For queries of an administrative nature, please contact Law School Reception.

After the release of the final grades for the course, feedback may be sought on examination performance in accordance with the School’s policy ( https://law.uq.edu.au/files/7645/TL14-Feedback-on-final-examinations.pdf ).

Deferred Examinations ( mid-semester and end of semester )

Where a student does not attend an examination, the student will in the normal course of events receive zero marks for the examination.

In accordance with Section 6 of PPL 3.10.11 Examinations – Procedures , permission to sit a deferred examination will only be granted in the event of a certified medical condition or other exceptional circumstances which prevented attendance at the examination. Note – except in truly exceptional circumstances, a student who attends whole or part of the original examination will not be eligible for a deferred examination.

Requests for deferral of an examination must be submitted online via mySI-net (myRequests tab) within 5 calendar days following the date of the examination. Supporting documentation must be attached to the request.

Deferred mid-semester examinations will be held in the period nominated by the School for the holding of mid-semester examinations. Deferred end of semester examinations will be held during the University’s deferred examination period.

Students should refer to my.UQ for further information relating to eligibility and how to request a deferred examination.

University Policies and Guidelines

Placement Courses

Students on a placement course – also known as a work placement, internship, industry study, industry experience, clinical practice, clinical placement, practical work, practicum, fieldwork, teaching practice – should refer to the University policy, Placement Courses ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.10.04-placement-courses ) for detailed information.

Working with Children

Students whose studies include a professional/work placement, internship, clinical practice, teaching practice or other similar activity which involves them in regular contact with children should refer to the University policy, Working with Children Check - "blue card" ( http://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/1.60.07-working-children ) to find out how to apply for a ‘blue card’.

Students with a Disability

Any student with a disability who may require alternative academic arrangements, including assessment, in the course/program is encouraged to seek advice at the commencement of the semester from a Disability Advisor at Student Services. Refer to the University policy, Students with a Disability (Disability Action Plan) ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.08-alternative-academic-arrangements-students-disability ) and to the policy on Special Arrangements for Examinations for Students with a Disability ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.50.09-arrangements-reasonable-adjustments-examinations-students-disability#Procedures ).

While it is the responsibility of the relevant faculty to liaise with professional and registration bodies regarding the acceptability of any adjustment to an academic program, the University Health Service can arrange appropriate advice and assistance on personal and public health issues.

Occupational Health and Safety

Undergraduate and Postgraduate Students should be familiar with the University policies on occupational health and safety in the laboratory ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/2.30.14-occupational-health-and-safety-laboratory ).

Fitness to Practise

Students enrolled in programs and courses that include a practical placement component (also known as work integrated learning, clinical immersion, clinical placement, clinical practice, externship, fieldwork, industry experience, industry study, internship, practicum, teaching practice, work placement) should refer to the University policy Fitness to Practise ( https://ppl.app.uq.edu.au/content/3.30.14-fitness-practise ) for detailed information on issues of competence and on management of concerns relating to patient and public safety.

Other
School of Law
Guidelines

Responsibilities of Students

Students are expected to take responsibility for their own learning and academic development. This includes:

devoting sufficient time and effort to your studies to make and maintain adequate progress,

preparing for lectures and tutorials,

reading prescribed materials,

undertaking such independent study as necessary to reach an understanding of the materials,

completion and submission of assessment items on time,

compliance with School, Faculty, and University rules.

Student Workload

For each 2 unit course students are expected to undertake at least 7 – 8 hours per week of independent study in addition to the time spent in lectures and tutorials.

Student and Staff Communication

If you email a staff member directly, you must include in your email your name, your student number and the name of the course you are enquiring about. Courtesy is always appreciated.

If you telephone a staff member directly and leave a message on their voicemail, you must provide contact details other than a mobile telephone number except where this is absolutely unavoidable.

While the use of telephone and email is acceptable for brief communications with a staff member, you should not expect detailed responses to questions about the law. These questions should be raised on the discussion board, in tutorial sessions or in staff consultation sessions.

Law School Website

Students should refer to the Law School website for more information including enrolment information and important dates; student announcements; news and events; information on Law School prizes and scholarships etc – http://www.law.uq.edu.au .

Previous 5. Assessment

Next Summary
course_introduction:
LAWS1100 Business Law provides an introduction to the legal rules regulating business in Australia today. Throughout the Course, students will explore how to use those legal rules to solve simple, everyday, legal problems. You will learn about the Australian legal system, the law of torts, the law of contract, consumer protection law (the ACL), intellectual property law and business structures. Your final grade will be based upon your level of understanding of and participation in the course, determined by reference to your marks for a multiple-choice question mid-semester exam, tutorial preparation and participation, a hand-in submission to an ILAC problem question and a final written exam based on ILAC problem questions.

Course Changes in Response to Previous Student Feedback

The tutorial program has been changed in order to better develop each students' ability to solve legal problems and to better prepare students for the mid semester and final examination.

The 20% Problem Set Scenario (ILAC question) was introduced in 2017 in order to help students to better develop their ability to answer legal problems using the ILAC method, prior to the final examination.

graduate_attributes.intro: Successfully completing this course will contribute to the recognition of your attainment of the following UQ (Undergrad Pass) graduate attributes:

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A1 . A comprehensive and well-founded knowledge in the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A4 . An understanding of how other disciplines relate to the field of study.

attributes.group: A . IN-DEPTH KNOWLEDGE OF THE FIELD OF STUDY
attributes.attribute: A5 . An international perspective on the field of study.
B1 . The ability to collect , analyse and organise information and ideas and to convey those ideas clearly and fluently , in both written and spoken forms .
B2 . The ability to interact effectively with others in order to work towards a common outcome.
B3 . The ability to select and use the appropriate level , style and means of communication .
B4 . The ability to engage effectively and appropriately with information and communication technologies .
C1 . The ability to work and learn independently .
C3 . The ability to generate ideas and adapt innovatively to changing environments.
C4 . The ability to identify problems , create solutions , innovate and improve current practices .
D1 . The ability to define and analyse problems .
D2 . The ability to apply critical reasoning to issues through independent thought and informed judgement .
D3 . The ability to evaluate opinions , make decisions and to reflect critically on the justifications for decisions.
E1 . An understanding of social and civic responsibility .
E2 . An appreciation of the philosophical and social contexts of a discipline.
E4 . A knowledge and respect of ethics and ethical standards in relation to a major area of study.
E5 . A knowledge of other cultures and times and an appreciation of cultural diversity .

other_learning_activities_information:
The Function of Lectures

Lectures are a forum where, within an analytical framework offered by the lecturer, students and lecturer can:

explore concepts of law relevant to the topic,

explore how the particular concepts of law work, and relate to other concepts learned,

identify the central conceptual, intellectual, and doctrinal questions raised by the material under consideration.

Lectures are not for:

delivering an exclusive and exhaustive account of the material that the student needs to learn, or

reading aloud material that students can read for themselves.

The Function of Tutorials

Tutorials offer students an interactive forum within which to explore in more detail and in greater depth the issues introduced in lectures and to engage with the tutorial staff and other students in exploring those issues. Tutorials also offer students an opportunity to develop their skills in legal reasoning and argument.

As an interactive learning forum, the tutorial is dependent upon all students having prepared the material to be considered and being prepared to engage in debate.

As a new incentive, Tutorials this Semester will also be used to give students extensive feedback on 2 x Problem Set Questions (1 graded and 1 ungraded). There will also be a practice 10 question multiple-choice exam (similar to the Mid-Semester Examination) held in Tutorial 2.

Previous 3. Resources

Next 5. Assessment
course_grading:
Grade X: No assessable work received.

Grade 1, Fail: Fails to demonstrate most or all of the basic requirements of the course:

0% - 19%

Grade 2, Fail: Demonstrates clear deficiencies in understanding and applying fundamental concepts; communicates information or ideas in ways that are frequently incomplete or confusing and give little attention to the conventions of the discipline:

20% - 47%

Grade 3, Fail: Demonstrates superficial or partial or faulty understanding of the fundamental concepts of the field of study and limited ability to apply these concepts; presents undeveloped or inappropriate or unsupported arguments; communicates information or ideas with lack of clarity and inconsistent adherence to the conventions of the discipline:

48% - 49%

Grade 4, Pass: Demonstrates adequate understanding and application of the fundamental concepts of the field of study; develops routine arguments or decisions and provides acceptable justification; communicates information and ideas adequately in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

50% - 64%

Grade 5, Credit: Demonstrates substantial understanding of fundamental concepts of the field of study and ability to apply these concepts in a variety of contexts; develops or adapts convincing arguments and provides coherent justification; communicates information and ideas clearly and fluently in terms of the conventions of the discipline:

65% - 74%

Grade 6, Distinction: As for 5, with frequent evidence of originality in defining and analysing issues or problems and in creating solutions; uses a level, style and means of communication appropriate to the discipline and the audience:

75% - 84%

Grade 7, High Distinction: As for 6, with consistent evidence of substantial originality and insight in identifying, generating and communicating competing arguments, perspectives or problem solving approaches; critically evaluates problems, their solutions and implications:

85% +
late_submission:
Where a non-examination item of assessment (eg essays, assignments etc) is not submitted by the due date and time, a penalty will be levied at the rate of 10% of the marks available for the assessment item per day that the assessment is overdue. The date and time of submission will be deemed to be that noted on the electronic submission.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 4pm on Monday, assessment submitted after 4pm Monday but before 4pm on Tuesday will receive a 10% penalty.

Assessment submitted more than 5 days after the due date and time will receive zero marks.

Example: Where a piece of assessment is due at 4pm on the 10th, any work submitted after 4pm on the 15th will receive zero marks.

If the 5th day following the due date is a Saturday or a Sunday, then the date and time of submission will be the time the assessment was electronically submitted.

Example: A piece of assessment is due at 4pm on Monday the 9th. In the normal course of events, to receive any marks, a student must submit their assessment electronically by 4pm on Saturday the 14th (the 5th day following the due date) at the latest.

No extension granted may exceed 14 days.

For full details, students should refer to the Guidelines for Applying for an Extension . Except in exceptional circumstances, extensions for assessment will only be considered in respect of a certified medical condition . Applications for extension (whether they be medical or exceptional circumstances) shall be made by the due date and time for the assessment unless the exceptional circumstance or medical condition is such that the student cannot reasonably be expected to have applied prior to the due date.
